data.title: CTO Realty Q1: High Quality REIT With Growth Potential
data.description:
I rate CTO Realty Growth, Inc. (NYSE: NYSE:CTO) a Buy, for income focused investors interested in real estate investment trusts. The REIT pays a quarterly dividend yielding 8.6% and goes ex-dividend on approximately 6/13/2025. CTO is benefiting from a robust economy in the U.S., and its proven management team is achieving results. In the recent Q1 earnings call, management confirmed robust leasing activity and strategic acquisitions to grow the portfolio. The portfolio is now 93.8% leased, which increases the ability to collect rent and highlights the ability of management to maintain the properties.

The most important factors that influence my opinion of the stock are

  • Professional, proven management team with history of high performance
  • Quality properties with high quality tenants such as Whole Foods, Dicks Sporting Goods, and The Capital Grille restraint chain
  • Growing rentable square footage by 19% CAGR
  • Growing Enterprise Value by 26% CAGR.

Seeking Alpha Quant rates the REIT a Hold 3.11 (as of 5/9/25), earning an A for valuation and growth. This is most likely driven by the market volatility driven by the tariff announcement on April 2nd, 2025. The market reacted, causing price pressure downward and prices are still being tested with day-to-day announcements.

Retail Based Property REIT

Headquartered in Winter Park, Florida, CTO Realty Growth, Inc. is a publicly traded real estate investment trust that owns and operates a portfolio of high-quality, retail-based properties located primarily in higher growth markets in the United States. CTO also externally manages and owns a meaningful interest in Alpine Income Property Trust, Inc. (NYSE: PINE), a publicly traded net lease REIT.

The shopping center REIT is focused on open-air centers in fast growing metropolitan statistical areas (MSAs) within Georgia, Texas, Florida, and North Carolina. Tenants such as Whole Foods Market as seen in the chart below sign long-term leases on high quality properties.

CTO’s portfolio has 5.2 million rentable square feet with an implied cap rate of 7.9%. This square footage has been built up over the years as the management team selects new properties and disposes of unwanted properties in an effort to grow and upgrade the holdings. A cap rate of almost 8% is very healthy in my view.

As you can see from the pictures below, CTO has a portfolio that attracts high quality tenants such as Publix, PNC Bank, The Capital Grille restaurant, Dicks Sporting Goods, and Nordstrom Rack.

Q1 2025 Earnings Beat

On May 1, the company declared Q1 2025 earnings, with the following financial highlights:

  • EPS achieve $0.01 which beats estimates by $0.27.
  • Revenue of $35.81 million increased 27.3% year on year, beat estimates by $556.3K
  • Portfolio was 93.8% leased and 91% occupied.
  • Core FFO was $14.4 million, up $3.7 million vs. quarter year ago.
  • Net debt to EBITDA was 6.6x, with $140 million in liquidity at the end of the quarter.

Overall, this was a healthy quarter demonstrating management’s ability to strategically add square footage to the portfolio and manage what it has to be fully leased. They worked to re-lease the space after some tenants leave and finally, CTO maintains a financially healthy balance sheet with ample liquidity at the end of the quarter. In my view, this management team stands out as being highly professional, active asset managers. They are generating revenues and profits while building for the future.

Performance To Date

$10,000 invested in CTO over the past 5 years would have doubled, growing to $23,040. This outperformed the sector index using Vanguard’s Real Estate Index Fund ETF (VNQ) as a proxy.

Valuation

CTO is increasing in value over time as shown in the chart below. Enterprise value is estimated at $1.3 billion in Q1 2025. This represents a 26% compound annual growth rate (CAGR) since Q4 2020, which is robust in my view. They are continually making strategic acquisitions, which increases the square footage of available rental space. From Q4 2020, CTO has increased their portfolio from 2.5 million square feet to 5.2 million in 2025.

CTO also points out the REIT has an attractive valuation and compelling dividend yield. From the chart below, its peers average annualized dividend yield is 4.6% vs. CTO at 7.9%. Similarly, CTO’s estimated 2025 core funds from operations (FFO) multiple is lower than the majority of peers at 10.5x.

The peers listed in the chart above include Acadia Realty Trust (AKR), Federal Realty Investment Trust (FRT), Whitestone REIT (WSR), Urban Edge Properties (UE), Brixmor Property Group Inc. (BRX), Kite Realty Group Trust (KRG), American Assets Trust Inc. (AAT).

Outlook

The future for CTO is especially bright now. Interest rates are at a level where they can adequately finance properties to build square footage and their balance sheet is healthy, generating positive FFO, which drives stock value.

On the Q1 Earnings call, management reaffirmed its full-year 2025 core FFO guidance of $1.80 to $1.86 and AFFO guidance of $1.93 to $1.98. The company expects its leasing activity will have a net positive effect as a growth driver for 2026. CTO continues to focus on strategic acquisitions in their targeted MSAs in southeast and southwest of the U.S. Management highlighted the attractive pipeline of opportunities in the current environment.

Risks To Rating

One of the main risks to retail space is the financial health of the tenant. If they go bankrupt, the landlords need to spend Capex to release the space.

While there may be risks to certain tenants because of U.S. tariff policies, CTO is positioned as having high quality properties and a diversified tenant base. This means if some of the tenants are affected, it won’t affect all of them. Also, if those particular tenants need to leave, CTO will be able to release the space because it is desirable.

Bottom Line

I rate CTO a Buy, for income focused investors interested in real estate investment trusts. The high-quality portfolio of retail shopping centers in the southeast and southwest of the US attracts high quality tenants such as Whole Foods, Dicks Sporting Goods, and The Capital Grille restaurant.

CTO currently pays a quarterly dividend yielding 8.6% and goes ex-dividend on approximately 6/13/2025. Management reaffirmed its 2025 guidance on the latest Q1 earnings call beating earnings estimates. The current portfolio is 93.8% leased, which is highly encouraging for rent collection. CTO also discussed strategic acquisitions being closed in the quarter and also projected throughout the year which will impact growth for 2026.

Overall, the most important factors that influence my opinion of the REIT are a professional management team, proven strategy, and a healthy financial status. The team is growing rentable square footage at a rate of 19% per year, which helps to drive enterprise value of 26% per year.

Risks to the REIT include tenant financial status and the impact of tariffs on some tenants. However, the portfolio of tenants is diversified as is the locations of properties in the various MSAs.

data.title: Warner Bros. Discovery: When Growth Outweighs Decline
data.description:
The last article (and many before) have concentrated on the turnaround effort and the fact that the growth story of Warner Bros. Discovery (NASDAQ:WBD) is finally beginning. This management is two years behind the original schedule because they found a lot of issues that needed fixing first. That two years was two years longer than the market had patience for. This is shown by all the rumors flying about splitting the company into two for an immediate profit, when there's probably a lot more profits if management is allowed to follow their plans. That may or may not involve a split down the road. But now, the growth of desirable business will dominate the quarterly reports from now on.

There's still the businesses that are in decline. But with each decline, they become less significant. Far more important is the wise use of the cash flow from the declining business before that business is gone.

The last article also noted that John Malone sold puts right after the spinoff to support the board. People like John Malone do not get "on board" with an idea like this unless they can make enough money for the risk taken. Typically, that return is a triple on the original investment over five years. The move to split the companies demonstrates that Mr. Market has given up on that profit idea. But this management has considerable backing from well-heeled investors that typically think long term.

Comstock

An example of this would be the investment Jerry Jones (owner of the Dallas Cowboys) made in Comstock (CRK). Jerry Jones invested roughly a billion of his money in Comstock before the challenges of fiscal year 2020. At one point you could invest at about half the price Jerry Jones paid. But who was interested at those natural gas prices?

Clearly, Jerry Jones now has a sizable profit on his investment, but many investors are only getting interested in the stock now when they could have gotten it for $4 a share had they had the patience that Jerry Jones has.

The latest quarterly report talks about a discovery the company made next to its own "backyard" that has a good chance of remaking the company and sending that stock price even higher.

While there's still good money to be made, Jerry Jones will again make a whole lot more money than the market because he invested in a situation when no one else would and he has held on until conditions have become much more favorable.

Few investors did what Jerry Jones did even if they followed him into Comstock back when he initially invested. That initial roughly 50% drop in the stock price would have terrified them into selling at breakeven or a small profit.

This demonstrates the difficulty of executing a contrarian investment strategy.

Warner Bros. Discovery Earnings

Now, with this earnings announcement, management is finally beginning the growth strategy that they wanted to begin back when the acquisition was made. But John Malone, as was the case with Jerry Jones, now has an investment worth about 50% less than when he sold those puts. Anyone who thinks that John Malone has given up on making the original profit goal has another thought coming. Understand that both Jerry Jones and John Malone will tell you their record is far from perfect. But the home runs far outweigh the other results.

The EBITDA comparison for the first quarter squeaked out an insignificant positive comparison. What a lot of investors seem to want to see is steady EBITDA growth.

Streaming

However, the stock may react strongly in the near future to streaming growth because this part of the company will likely be central to the future of the company.

Streaming posted its second-best EBITDA quarter ever (post-acquisition). Management further excited Mr. Market by forecasting sizable EBITDA growth for the fiscal year to record EBITDA.

Market Concerns

Mr. Market was concerned about declining ARPU despite management assurances that the decline was largely due to the entrance into lower ARPU markets at first.

There was also, of course, questions about the bundling affecting the ARPU as well. Those concerns partially offset the positive effects of a climbing adjusted EBITDA.

Also helping to calm the market concerns with the growth in subscribers shown above.

One thing reiterated during the conference call, the earnings reported, and many times in the past was the fact that profitable growth here was the key now. The conversion to free cash flow is also an important measure for management as well.

Studios

The studios segment results were overshadowed by some current (large) hits that will affect the second quarter results. This division is likely to have highly variable results because consistent movie results are rare.

Both games and movies have very volatile results. Hits often benefit a quarter and then the quarterly comparison in the following year is likely to be a challenge compared to market expectations.

In this case, despite the absence of a big hit in the first quarter, EBITDA grew as management gained control of expenses.

Cash Flow

With any leveraged transaction like this one, management prioritizes free cash flow to repay debt until debt levels meet the original goal.

Now the first quarter is generally the weakest cash flow quarter for the company.

This is the second year in a row that the company posted positive free cash flow after the first (first quarter after the acquisition) time showed a big negative free cash flow for that quarter.

That gets the company off to a very good start in what is seasonally the weakest quarter for free cash flow.

Summary

John Malone and Jerry Jones probably made their fortunes by thinking long term that had a contrarian investment philosophy component. If one were to agree with John Malone's original profit forecast, then there's every chance for him to still make a triple as Jerry Jones did after the value of Jerry Jones' initial investment was roughly cut in half first.

Many investors pat themselves on the back with a 20% to 30% return while thanking their lucky starts they made it past that initial decline without taking that loss.

That's why this remains a strong buy. Even if John Malone falls short in his original goal, there is every chance that the investor can do well from the current price. In fact, I still believe it's very possible to do well from John Malone's original cost. However, smaller investors now have a chance to do better with less downside risk.

The execution of the streaming expansion plans is now well underway. Meanwhile, movies have two hits before this management team has movies released that it created from start to finish.

As a result, the stock price could surprise to the upside considerably despite a lot of uncertainty.

Risks

This article assumes that the threat of tariffs and trade wars are negotiating tools that will be replaced with something constructive, well communicated, and permanent. There's no assurance that will happen though.

The relatively fast cash flow turnaround for an acquisition of the size made here makes this management quite rare in any industry. A loss of key personnel of such management could prove to be a real challenge to replace.

There's a lot of hope for "hit movies" now that management has what it wants. But the movie business has proven problematic to many managements in the past. Consistency in the movie business and video games is a very challenging goal to achieve.

data.title: Itaú Unibanco: Profitability Without Surprises, But Without Failures
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Itaú Unibanco (NYSE:ITUB) is a pillar of the Latin American banking system, since it not only has a solid presence in Brazil, but also carries out important operations in the region. The decisions it has made in recent years, such as the sale of operations in Argentina to Banco Macro (BMA), show that the bank targets markets with greater long-term predictability, in a scenario of high regional volatility.

ITUB’s further growth opportunities are enhanced by the current moderate growth expectation for Brazil tied with contained inflation and a potential reduction in the Selic interest rate. These factors are very likely to boost demand, especially in the digital and retail banking and consumer segments.

Furthermore, its presence in countries such as Chile, Colombia, Uruguay and Paraguay gives it a very relevant advantage, since it allows it to diversify risks and take advantage of the gradual economic recovery of these markets.

At an operational level, I consider that ITUB stands out for its efficiency and its strong commitment to digitalization. In this way, Itaù shows that it does not just want to be a traditional bank, but that it positions itself as a very strong competitor against fintechs, which are rapidly gaining ground.

Itaú has reinforced its profitability in Q1 2025 with an exceptional recurring revenue performance coupled with strong operational efficiency. The company continues to command one of the highest positions in the LATAM region with an ROE of 22.5% making it one of the most profitable banks in the region, showcasing the solid returns supported by a well managed cost structure.

Personally, I have a hard time finding banks with this level of operational efficiency, quality of execution, and long-term vision in the region. I do not expect Itaú to surprise with explosions of growth, but I do expect it to maintain this profitable stability that is so lacking in Latin America.

In that sense, I see it as a very attractive option for a long-term position in portfolios seeking banking exposure to emerging markets, with solid fundamentals and a clear strategy.

Overview

Itaú Unibanco (ITUB) is one of the pillars of the Latin American financial system. With a dominant presence in Brazil and operations in several countries in the region,it continues to be a central player in the continent's economic life.

Over the last year, it made key strategic decisions, such as the sale of its subsidiary in Argentina to Banco Macro (BMA) – which I analyzed in a previous article – an exit that had been maturing for some time and that, in my opinion, was a natural step. In a context as volatile and regulated as Argentina in 2023, where bank profitability had been elusive, Itaú chose to concentrate on more predictable and scalable markets.

Brazil remains the bank's operational heart, and this is no small detail. The Brazilian economy is projected with moderate growth for 2025, around 2% according to the Central Bank, with inflation contained around 5.5% and a Selic rate that could continue to decline if signs of disinflation consolidate.

ITUB, with its huge customer base and technological strength, is so well positioned to capitalize on this improvement in demand, especially in the consumer and digital banking segments.

It is also worth mentioning that Itaú is not limited only to Brazil. Its presence in Chile, Colombia, Uruguay and Paraguay gives it a very valuable regional diversification today. In a region that is going through very different years in terms of monetary policy, inflation and growth, the ability to balance geographies is a structural advantage. This year, for example, a slow but sustained recovery is expected in several South American countries, with lower inflationary pressures and some recovery in credit, something that could benefit well-positioned banks such as Itaú.

From an operational perspective, the bank continues to invest heavily in digitalization, something I have been following for some time. They have invested heavily in digital channels, remote onboarding, and improvements to the customer experience. They are not simply competing with traditional banks; they are building an offering that also challenges the most aggressive fintechs, but with the advantage of scale, trust, and a long track record of solidity.

For me, Itaú isn't a bank that promises big surprises or disruptive leaps, but it is one that inspires confidence. It's an entity that, amid so much global and regional uncertainty, continues to operate with logic, efficiency, and a long-term vision. And that, in these times, is saying a lot.

Q1 2025

Itaú started the year on the right foot, and there is no doubt why it is the most profitable and solid bank in Latin America. In Q1 2025 it had a recurring result of R$ 11.1B (+13.9% YoY), well above what the market expected, which shows that the bank continues to expand its business in a healthy manner.

An important part of this good performance is due to the sustained increase in income. Operating income was R$44.5B, 10.4% higher than Q1 2024. The most notable financial margin (net interest) of R$30.3B (+12.8% YoY) was driven by loan growth and stronger spreads in a context of credit recovery.

On the other hand, income from services, insurance and pensions also increased, while expenses grew below income, allowing ITUB to maintain good operational efficiency dynamics. In fact, the efficiency index stood at 38.1%, which confirms the bank's ability to generate more income with a controlled cost structure, something key in the current competitive context.

From a profitability perspective, Itaú stood out. ROE was 22.5%, an exceptional level not only within the Brazilian financial system but globally. Few entities in the world manage to combine size, scale, profitability, and efficiency in this way. And while ROE declined slightly compared to previous quarters, it remains above 20%, reflecting a structurally high and sustainable profitability.

Valuation

Itaú's valuation indicates mixed results relative to the sector. The P/E TTM ratio is 9.13x, 19.34% below the sector median of 11.31x, and the P/E FWD also shows a negative gap, at 18.38% below the sector average.

Meanwhile, analyzing the valuation multiples, it is possible to observe that ITUB presents weakness in its P/B TTM of 1.98x, which is 66.88% above the sector, and a similar, although more moderate, FWD ratio of 69.91%.This could indicate that the market is overvaluing the bank's book value.

Analyzing profitability, the bank presents a ROE of 20.48%, well above (almost double) the sector average (10.42%), which marks greater efficiency in generating profits from own resources.

In terms of growth, the results show that ITUB appears to be in a moderating phase. Revenue growth is 9.00%, above the industry average (6.30%), although the FWD results indicate a sharp slowdown to just 2.70%, which could indicate a more challenging environment for future expansion, especially when compared to the average growth over the past five years (9.61%).

EPS projections also show a slowdown, with 7.74% growth projected for the coming years, well below historical growth.

Furthermore, the net income margin is 30.27%, above the industry median of 22.70%, suggesting that the company has a solid ability to convert revenue into net profit.

Risks

One of the most evident risks when investing in Itaú is its strong exposure to the Brazilian economy. Although Brazil is an enormous market with potential, it is also very volatile and sensitive to political, fiscal or inflationary shocks. While the bank has been able to navigate past crises, a deep economic slowdown or an aggressive rate hike could hit credit demand and deteriorate the quality of its portfolio.

I'm also concerned about regulatory risk. Latin American banks operate under regulatory frameworks that can change quickly and sometimes unpredictably. In Brazil, changes in benchmark interest rates, capital requirements, or even public lending policies can affect banking margins. Not that this happens all the time, but when it does, the effects can be felt strongly on the balance.

Another point to take into account is the growing competition in the fintech sector. ITUB has advanced its digitalization and has a very strong brand, but new digital players are gaining ground, especially among younger users. Although the bank has the financial muscle to compete, it is still a front that can erode profitability in certain segments if it does not adapt quickly.

Finally, there is the FX risk for investors looking from outside Brazil. Although ITUB is listed in New York, its revenue is in Brazilian reais. Any currency devaluation directly impacts returns in dollars. This is something that cannot be ignored, especially if one seeks to preserve value in hard currencies over the long term. Therefore, although Itaú seems to me to be a well-managed bank, these risks must always be kept in mind.

Conclusions

After an in-depth review of Itaú Unibanco's profile, it's quite clear to me why it remains one of the jewels of the Latin American financial system. It's not a bank that promises spectacular surprises or explosive growth, but it does deliver something that's invaluable in this context: consistency, efficiency, and long-term vision.

Its financial strength, its strategic focus on more predictable markets, and its ability to adapt to the digital revolution position it as a player that not only withstands cycles, but navigates them intelligently.

Personally, I recommend buying ITUB. I'm confident in its track record, its sustained profitability, and its well-executed technological commitment. In a market like Latin America, where stability is a scarce asset, Itaú offers exactly that: predictability with returns. For me, it's a position that adds balance and quality to any emerging markets-oriented portfolio.

data.title: Sketches On The Boundaries Of The Efficient Market Hypothesis
data.description:
Author's Prelude

This series of sketches is meant to jot down all the miscellaneous thoughts and discussion points I’ve had about the Efficient Market Hypothesis. It is not meant to be a coherent academic paper or an essay arguing a single point, but rather an attempt to establish some boundaries on where the EMH applies and where it does not. This is a very ambitious goal as ”the market” is composed of tens of thousands of numerous interconnected markets for individual commodities and securities. So at best I can write ”sketches” in broad strokes. My hope is that I can get some clarity for myself (and hopefully you) on this topic by writing down these sketches.

There are a total of 7 sketches, each part about 800-900 words long on average. Just a heads up that this article is on the long side.

1) Forms Of The Efficient Market Hypothesis & The Joint Hypothesis Problem

Eugene Fama’s 1970 review paper delineated the three forms of market efficiency and highlighted various pieces of evidence for and against it that had been published at the time. The three forms are:

  • Weak form: Market prices account for all price history information, and it is impossible to earn trading profits on any trading system that is solely based on price history. In other more statistical terms this is known as the ”random walk” thesis.
  • Semi-strong form: Market prices are weak form efficient, as well as accounting for all publicly available information about the security. This form of the EMH suggests that it is impossible to reliably outperform the market on using solely public information as a basis for trading decisions.
  • Strong form: Market prices are semi-strong form efficient, as well as accounting for all information that is privately held. So this form of EMH states that it is not possible to outperform the market even using insider information or given monopolistic access to an information source.

In common parlance EMH is often boiled down to the maxim ”it is highly unlikely that you will beat the market”. This is a common experience among market participants of various skill levels, but unfortunately it loses the nuance provided by the three different forms that are a function of the information set assumed by each form. For this rest of this writeup if I 1 state ”EMH” without stating which form, I will be referring to this common parlance maxim.

Unfortunately, it is not possible to quantify or measure market efficiency. One can only develop a gut feeling for it. This is because in order to quantify it, a model of market prices must be used that is a ”correct” model against which all market prices can be compared to judge for efficiency or inefficiency. But, since all such models must be constructed and validated on some empirical basis, such a model cannot exist. This is the ”joint hypothesis problem” - a test of market efficiency is also a test of the price model used to evaluate market efficiency. Hence, a discussion of market efficiency must degenerate to essay writing rather than be based on statistical tests; hence these written sketches.

2) Logical Truth Value Vs. Working Assumption

There is no question that the EMH as a proposition stated across all markets simultaneously is flatly wrong. However, I consider this a deeply uninteresting conclusion, akin to the joke of answering ”42” to ”what is the meaning of life and of the universe?”. This is because some markets may be more efficient than others, and different people may treat the market differently: they consider the market they surf to be ”efficient” or ”inefficient” as a function of their self-assessment of their ability to surf that market, as well as the business and value proposition they represent if they manage other people’s money. The interesting question is delineating the boundary between what markets are efficient and what markets are not efficient. As a simple example, large cap developed equity markets approach the ideal of market efficiency more than do small cap and illiquid markets. I will necessarily write these sketches from my own perspective, so my conclusions will likely differ from yours if you have a different skill set than mine.

To illustrate this point, I’ll point out Burton Malkiel and Jim Simons. Burton Malkiel is an economist and was a long time board member of Vanguard, a leading provider of index funds. Malkiel is a proponent of EMH, which predicates Vanugard’s business model that assumes that the most investors (both retail and institutional) cannot beat the market, and so would be better off investing in an index fund. Clearly, Malkiel’s business alignment and proposition must agree with his largely positive espoused views on EMH. On the other hand Jim Simons is a mathematician who owns and runs the quant fund Renaissance Technologies, which is predicated on gathering information in quantities far larger than other participants can hope to get, in quantities far larger than other participants want, and processing it more quickly than anyone else. The outsized rate of return of their Medallion fund is a clear counterexample against EMH, and unsurprisingly, Simons publicly states that the EMH is wrong - this is his working assumption.

Since investors exist on a spectrum of skill levels and ability to access information, whether to regard the market they surf as efficient from their perspective or not is a private question that they can answer only for themselves. My hunch is that most people within the first 10-15 years (just over one full market cycle) of their experience on the markets should make 2 a decision on what working assumption they should make on EMH. I imagine that most people who start their investing career without assuming EMH and opting for buying and holding index funds chooses the red pill instead and tries to analyze and trade individual stocks for themselves. An investor’s relationship with the EMH is a fundamental question as it dictates the attitude one comes to market with when interacting with market participants.

A person with no concept of EMH who treats their own judgment as the sole measure of the true value of an investment is adopting the most arrogant ”smarter than thou” attitude possible towards a room full of strangers that they will likely never meet or talk to. On the other hand, a person with an awareness of EMH will walk into a market and adopt an attitude of humility, and be an avid listener and conversationalist and be receptive to understanding why the market has priced an investment with the price it has, before making any investment decisions. Even if the letter of EMH is wrong, I believe it is impossible to be a successful investor without making a judgment on their skill level relative to that of the market.

So you must be asking, who are you, Stephen Nemo, and what is your skill level? I am a (relatively) young person with about 10 years of active market experience, who experienced the COVID market collapse first hand and missed out on the bull market that followed it. I have done equity analysis for the past 10 years the ”proper” way - reading financial statements, annual and quarterly SEC filings, browsing investment forums for nuggets of information in comments, and occasionally having conversations with people who work for various companies. I am a retail investor who tries my best to do things the right way. I have a small amount of experience on the bond markets. I consider my circle of competence to be largely confined to BDCs, REITs, bank stocks, and corporate bonds. I also feel that I can make decisions about large cap stocks whose annual financial statements have been relatively ”clean” and devoid of noise. And yes, I will confirm that solo security analysis is a lot of painstaking detail work.

I’ll be honest, despite my best efforts I’ve lagged the S&P500. But I won’t tell you by how much. I am about to lose my beginner status, and by writing this article I am gaining my own awareness of just how smart market participants collectively are. And I’m definitely guilty of not being aware of and / or purposefully ignoring EMH in the past, in the name of red-pilling myself. I was young and chose the path of pain, and now I am in the process of deciding if relative to me, the markets are efficient. The rest of this article is what I’ve learned and things that must feed into my decision. The punchline: I wish I had the knowledge to write these sketches a lot earlier, as it would have saved me a lot of sunk cost time and pain. And my net worth would be quite a bit bigger. But I can still choose to continue to redpill myself - maybe I’m just not yet good enough to do well but I will be later. That will be my private choice.

3) Quantitative Fund Strategies

I’m far from being an expert on the activities of quantitative funds - I’m aware of just two activities out of the many that they do, but this should be enough to begin to comment on their relationship to EMH. The first activity I’ll mention is market making. Firstly, market making is a highly profitable operation I quote from a 2021 article by a writer at Cornell ”... We know this to be true because market-making firms are incredibly profitable. According to the Quartz article below, Citadel Securities and Virtu Financial, two market-making firms that control roughly 22% of all U.S. equities trading volume, generated over $10 billion in net trading revenue and completed more transactions than the New York Stock Exchange. If markets were typically in equilibrium, such revenue numbers would not be attainable as market makers earn no profit in perfect competition.”

Let’s do a tiny bit of math. The US stock market in 2021 was worth about $52 trillion. On average, 0.5% of it is traded every day, and since Citadel and Virtu had 22% of market volume it means they earned $10 billion of net trading revenue on ∼$14 trillion of trading volume if we take a year to have 252 trading days. This is an average gross profit margin of 0.07%. However since relatively little capital is needed to run a market making business, this tiny gross profit transforms into a very large gross return on invested capital.

Market making involves setting bid and ask prices on a stock as a function of the order flow on that stock. This means that market makers leverage their privileged access to trading information in order to generate a trading profit. While the 0.07% gross margin is truly minuscule when compared to the gross margins of normal publicly traded companies, nevertheless this is may be construed as a violation of the letter of strong form market efficiency, albeit a very marginal one when judged on the basis of comparing gross profit and trading volume. However, on the other hand, an even more careful reading of the situation would state that the activity of providing liquidity to a market is a risk-taking activity earns its reward, so it is perhaps not entirely clear whether strong form market efficiency is strictly violated.

Quantitative firms only seem highly profitable because the amount of capital they deploy to capture this gross profit is vanishingly small - hence they seem to generate ”massive” returns on the surface. However, there are multiple market makers, and they all compete with each other in a perfect competition - it is the market structure of the industry that causes the bid-ask spread to be minuscule in equilibrium. And in order to win this minuscule gross return (but large ROIC), quantitative funds are in a race to hire new math & CS PhD talent, and are in a perpetual arms race in investing in data gathering and communications infrastructure and computing power. All of this takes as given that they can invest and manage their pools of requisite human, physical, and financial capital. Data travels at the speed of light and a difference of mere feet of distance to the exchange can determine who wins the latency race. Think twice before you choose to participate in a perfect competition - this quasi violation of strong form market efficiency costs a pretty penny to start up and run.

The other activity I am aware of is pairs trading. Namely, the tracking and trading of pairs of securities that have at least exhibited superficial statistical relationships with each other, and hopefully doing so with a fundamentally economic rationale. For instance, Ford and General Motors might be a pair: both are major auto companies in the US, both are leading manufacturers, and their products are similar in quality and type, and they target the same markets. Therefore they should have similar financial performance, and they should respond to macroeconomic forces similarly. Unsurprisingly the movements of their share prices are highly correlated with each other, and so if one of them diverges from this pattern, there is strong reason to believe that they might eventually converge. So the trade is to long one and short the other, and be market neutral, but earn a return from that convergence. This might be considered a violation of the letter of semi-strong market efficiency, or possibly weak form market efficiency, depending on your classification. A paper by Gatev et al. (2006) suggested in backtests that pairs trading in US equity markets from 1926 to 2002 returned on average an excess return (as compared with the return on treasury bills) of about 11% per year.

But to execute this strategy, as well as other statistical arbitrages, again one needs a lot of programming and data / computing infrastructure to handle the vast quantities of data involved, be able to cast a wide net over tens, if not hundreds or thousands of pairs of stocks, and have skilled employees who can manage and monitor the strategy. In short, there is a massive amount of capital - both financial and human - that is needed to execute on this. Like with how market making however, violating the letter of EMH is not without its price - in both cases, it require a large amount of capital, both financial and human to execute.

So to summarize, while quant firms are able to violate the letter of EMH, they do so in a way that requires enormous amounts of capital investment and compete with each other in a perfect competition. And to top it all off, the assets under management at quantitative firms are only a small fraction of the total assets of the market. So yes, while it is true that quantitative firms violate the letter of EMH, the size of the violation is in practice quite small relative to the size of the market and is very expensive to get. Think twice before you try to compete.

4) The Efficiency Of Warren Buffett

Contrary to popular opinion, I do not see Buffett as a creative genius who finds investment ideas via some sort of divine inspiration, but rather as a highly skilled operator of strategies that he learned from others. His career spans multiple decades - from the beginning of WWII for the US in 1942 when he placed his first stock trade to the present. During that time, I would argue that his investing strategy has changed over time to reflect the changing degree of efficiency of the US stock market.

Buffett’s early years as a stock trader were absorbed in technical analysis - the analysis of 5 price patterns to generate trading signals. He has consistently claimed in public that in his early years he read all the books in his local library on investing and tried all the strategies, but made no money for the first 11 or so years of his career. This was his informal test of weak market efficiency. Then he went to university and encountered Benjamin Graham. Back in those 1940s, the US stock market was arguably highly inefficient - the collective memory still contained the dramatic stock market crash of 1929 that wiped out fortunes and led to the Great Depression, which soured the public away from the stock market. During this period, one could have bought a stock for a P/E of 2, and sold it at a P/E of 3, which is a far cry from the competitive pricing of stocks in today’s market.

One strategy Buffett learned (I think from Ben Graham) was the ”net-net”, namely the buying of stocks that were trading at below the value of their current assets, and selling it once it converged in price. This is a very basic example of Buffett being more efficient than the market: why should a box that contains at least a $1 bill sell for just 75 cents? But it also underscores the dramatic inefficiency of the market back then. Nowadays, in the current hypercompetitive US stock market, net-net trades simply cannot be found. The few companies today that might ”count’ as a net-net are small mining exploration and biotech companies whose cash hoards come from equity raises and which are expected to spend down their cash hoard to nothing in the search of a viable product or mother lode.

In this same vein, Buffett early in his career was skilled at bringing information to the stock market. Back in the 1950s when information technology was not so advanced, when one had to search for hours in a library to find annual reports, while finding new information to being to market was a hard thing to do, clearing the bar for what counted as new information was also quite easy. Buffett has talked publicly of ”flipping pages” - reading Moody’s manuals - tens of thousands of pages of financial data on publicly traded companies, looking for deals. For years the best information source he had was Moody’s manuals, and for years reading their pages one by one was sufficient to have an informational edge on the market. Clearly that is no longer the case now that the internet is so good at disseminating information across all markets.

Later on, Buffett would meet Charlie Munger in 1959. Then Buffett would read Philip Fisher’s book Common Stocks and Uncommon Profits. I believe it was the combined influence of Munger’s advice and Fisher’s book that was the fuel for the rest of Buffett’s life. Munger’s advice was simple: instead of being willing to buy lousy (”cigar butt”) businesses at very low prices, buy fantastic businesses at fair prices. The idea is to pay less attention to buying $1 bills for 75 cents and instead look for businesses that have high profit margins, high returns on assets, and modest debt, that can invest and reinvest their capital to grow continuously. Coca-Cola, American Express, and Apple are iconic examples of this.

I believe Philip Fisher’s book filled in the gaps in Munger’s advice. Fisher advocated for an investment approach that emphasized the value of gathering information that is not readily available to the public, and in doing so would allow an investor who had that information to make judgments with to outperform, and thereby in theory have the potential to break the letter of the strong form of EMH. Fisher’s approach can be summarized in the form 6 of a 15-point checklist given here. Some of the points here can only be discovered by what Fisher called ”scuttlebutt”, namely speaking directly with the employees, managers, and customers of a business of interest. The points that I think that are relevant are here:

  • Does the company have an above average sales organization?
  • Does the company have outstanding labor and personnel relations?
  • Does the company have outstanding executive relations?
  • Does the company have a management of unquestionable integrity?

Beyond 10-K filings there are many other ways to find this kind of information in bits and snatches: Google, LinkedIn, Glassdoor, Twitter (X), etc., aside from conducting literal interviews and asking actual people. The idea is to break free from the confines of decisionmaking on public information and seek private information outside of the market that might not have been factored into the stock price. One example of an investment by Buffett that may have been informed by scuttlebutt was his investment in American Express after the ”Salad Oil” scandal. American Express was defrauded into issuing receipts for salad oil that did not exist, and the exposure of the fraud caused American Express stock to drop over 50%. Buffett noted that American Express consumers were still happily using their cards despite the onset of the scandal, and inferred that the American Express brand was still intact, and bought American Express stock. This act of using personal observation to profit could be construed as possibly violating the letter of strong form EMH. There are probably many other examples of Buffett’s decision making being influenced by observations of consumer behavior.

As an aside (but still related to the EMH topic), while all of these investments were going on, Buffett used the time in between major investments to conduct ”workouts”, which were short term investments that also took advantage of market mispricing. One such type of workout was merger arbitrage. To illustrate, suppose that company A is to be acquired by B next year. Suppose that A’s price is bid up by the market to 90% that of the final closing price. A merger arbitrageur could buy shares of A after the merger announcement and hold them until the acquisition is completed, and earn the difference. As merger arbitrage became more and popular, it became more and more competitive, to the point that returns to merger arbitrage have been competed closer to the risk free rate. Buffett used merger arbitrage (and other ”workouts”) as a substitute for holding treasury bills that had a higher expected rate of return.

However, I would reckon that returns to these workout strategies diminished by around 2000. This is evidence by my past factor analysis of Berkshire Hathaway stock that showed that Buffett had for various 5 year windows, Buffett had significant positive alpha despite inclusion of factors other than the market factor in a factor regression. My observation was that this alpha was strongest pre-2000, and diminished to near zero post 2000, either because Berkshire grew too large to benefit from these strategies or the returns to the strategies themselves dwindled as the market became more competitive. Buffett’s performance since the Great Financial Crisis of 2008 has been relatively lackluster - Berkshire has largely tracked the S&P500 since then. To summarize, I would suggest that Buffett was capable of being more efficient than the market up to around 2000, after which point he could at best track the overall market. This is partly due to Berkshire Hathaway’s size, but I also reckon it is because markets have simply become more competitive since the start of Buffett’s career.

A famous paper by Frazzini, Kabiller, and Pedersen titled ”Buffett’s Alpha” is a factor regression analysis of Buffett’s returns. It uses a linear regression to decompose the returns of Berkshire Hathaway in terms of risk factors. It showed that Buffett largely selected stocks for their exposure to the value factor, low-beta (low volatility) factor, and quality. This, combined with cheap leverage provided by insurance float, was the formula for Buffett’s success. So there is a hint here that Buffett may not necessarily have been more efficient than the market - he could have simply have been the first adopter of factor investing, and profited handsomely from it. Before anyone suggests that Buffett should be used as an example that it’s possible to systematically beat the market (violate EMH), I should remind them that most of Buffett’s outperformance occurred before the year 2000. And a quarter of a century has passed since then. Additionally because of Buffett's use of leverage, it would not be right to compare him directly with the S&P500, but rather a fair comparison would be the S&P500 levered up to Buffett's level of leverage.

There are many other big name value investors whose careers I could have mentioned - Guy Spier, Aswath Damodaran, Seth Klarman, Mohnish Prabrai, etc. But I think since they are younger (and less well known) and have shorter track records their tales are not as easy to tell. Seth Klarman is famously secretive. But there are some things that we can infer about him: he operates in a market more efficient that Buffett operated in, and he always gravitates to the segments of the market typical investors pay the least attention to. This means a willingness to make every industry part of one’s circle of competence - this is in opposition to Buffett sticking largely to his own self-declared circle of competence. Indeed Klarman may be an even more superpowered version of Buffett, albeit 30 years younger.

The fact is that as times change, so does the edge one need in order to beat the market. To close this section, it’s worth mentioning that all the great value investors have one thing in common: a love and passion for reading, for hours and hours a day. Could I sit down with a 10,000+ page Moody’s manual and read the whole thing page by page looking for bargains? I don’t think so. Additionally, I’d reckon that the assets under management of these superstar investors is maybe 2-3% of the total value of stocks in the stock market. Yes, while I agree that with a sufficiently analytical mind and enough reading it might be possible to ”beat the market”, consider the opportunity cost of this path: a lifetime could have been spent doing other things, such as giving personal attention to raising a family, enjoyment of art and nature, formal academic research, and other careers with more immediate human impact, etc.

5) The Active Investing Industry & The Retail Investor

So far I have mostly focused on the outperformance of the select few companies and investors who have the human, financial, and physical capital needed to violate the various forms of EMH and who are willing to accept the lifetime opportunity cost of running such a business. My best guess is that the assets under the management of these companies and superstar investors are no more than 3-5% of total assets under management. So what about everyone else?

It’s a trope that retail investors not only cannot match the market, but they also lag significantly behind the market. I won’t even bother citing or linking any sources - they are easily found by Google search. I think the general reason is a lack of recognition of the intensity of competition in the markets, a propensity to test the strength of weak form EMH via leveraged trading and option trading (betting on the news), a tendency to place credence in the predictive powers of technical analysis (technical indicators and charts), which is directly contradicted by weak form EMH and something Buffett discarded at around age 22 after many years of experimenting with it while a teenager. Additionally, retail investors have behavioral issues such as buying high and selling low, chasing hot funds, and timing the market.

A DALBAR study showed that from 2001 - 2021, the S&P500 index returned 10.7% per year, but the average retail fund investor only earned 7.1% per year on average. Another study by DALBAR showed that the average equity investor lagged the market by 6% a year over a 30 year period. In my opinion it is the recognition of just how difficult it is to violate EMH in all its forms (consider the financial and human capital investment that is needed, as described above) that should cause a retail investor to turn away from perpetually testing weak and semi-strong form EMH and towards buying and holding index funds for at least some fraction of their portfolio.

Another trope of asset management is that the vast majority of professional active managers are not able to beat their benchmark indices, and it is even less common for a professional active manager to consistently beat an index. Hence simply trusting a professional to manage money is not a great solution either. This leads into the next problem, which is how can one select for an active manager who can reliably beat the market? As far as I know, there simply is no answer to this. Given that the volatility of the stock market is about 18% per year, and that even a 1-2% annual outperformance from an active manager is a spectacular run, picking an outperforming active manager on the basis of their investment results should require a very long track record (on the order of multiple decades) in order for it to be statistically convincing - passing some sort of hypothesis test.

Enter the ”Bogleheads”, followers of Jack Bogle. The core of the Boglehead philosophy is that it is virtually impossible to beat the market by means available to retail investors, and so it is best to buy and hold index funds in the long run, by picking and sticking to some asset allocation that satisfies the investor’s preferences. The retail investor simply buys the market asset, and lets the active and superstar investors price their assets for them. All low level thinking about details of individual investments has been removed from investing, beyond understanding the characteristics of the asset classes in the portfolio and periodic rebalancing. And in theory, if the index funds picked are sufficiently low cost, given enough investing discipline and a long enough time horizon, a retail investor can finally reach the point of approaching collecting the returns of the market.

Note here that these retail investors and professional active investors are starting from a place of weakness: an unremarkable endowment of human capital (the sort of human capital that superstar managers have), and zero investment in physical capital and reputation needed to attract math & CS PhDs needed to run a quantitative fund. And hence note that in general, when the professional active managers, Bogleheads, and active retail investors are summed up, their average return can at most be equal to that of the market. This means that the set complement of this group (superstar investors and quant funds) must by exclusion and differences earn an above market rate of return. This is because of the tautology that states that the set of all market participants must on average earn the market return. And it’s quite clear (to me at least) that the barrier that separates these two sets of market participants is their choice of working assumption about whether they have the ability (human / financial / physical capital endowment) to beat the market. Whether and to what extent EMH is true is a personal decision.

6) The Future Efficiency Level Of The Market

I think the corporate bond market is probably the easiest to rate. Bond prices in equilibrium are generally determined by the risk free rate and a credit spread. The credit spread is a function of the perceived ability of a bond issuer to service the debt by paying the coupons, and the capacity to either pay down the principal or to refinance the bond by paying it off by issuing a new bond. All of the primary relevant information for this can be found in SEC filings, namely public information. Therefore corporate bond markets in equilibrium are almost certainly semi-strong form efficient.

As for the stock market, ChatGPT will likely be a gamechanger. It has the capacity to read thousands of pages of text and then create a summary report of that text within minutes. I recently fed the last ten past 10-K filings from American Express to ChatGPT’s deep research function, and it produced a nice 60 page summary out of 2,000 pages of SEC filings. Surely ChatGPT will be a tool every professional equity analyst will use to help them navigate the ocean of information in front of them, implying that markets in the future will probably be even more efficient than they were in the past. Knowledge that used to represent years of an analyst’s experience reading and interviewing people about a company can be replicated in minutes using ChatGPT. Since SEC filings are all highly material public information, I would consider the US stock market to be at least semi-strong form efficient in terms of future analysts’ ability to bring public information to market. However, of course I would have to make exceptions for the fact that pairs trading and market making are violations of weak and strong form EMH.

Two other violations of EMH by the stock market are worth mentioning: the momentum factor (violation of weak EMH), and the existence of stock market bubbles (violation of semi-strong EMH). Momentum - the propensity of assets that have provided a positive return in the past to provide a positive return in the short term future - is tricky to harvest, which incurs many trading costs. As far as I know, momentum funds that outperform the market do so at an annualized rate of 1% to 2% a year, and so to my knowledge picking momentum funds suffers from the same problem as picking outperforming active managers: the signal to noise ratio is biased towards noise.

Stock market bubbles are market-wide phenomena where the market sentiment becomes so euphoric and speculative that valuations of large parts of the stock market become unanchored from any rational deduction from publicly available information - hence they represent deviations away from the semi-strong form of market efficiency. Stock market bubbles are dramatic but in terms of the percentage of stock market time they have occupied since 1900, I’d reckon that the bubble leading up to the 1929 stock market crash and the bubble that led up to the Dot-com bubble crash represent about 5% or so of stock market history. This is to say that this violation of semi-strong market efficiency occurs for only a small fraction of stock market time. It is possible that stock market bubbles will happen again, but they will unlikely take on the form of being fueled by investing on margin (the 1929 crash) or technological speculation unanchored by any sane expectations of future cash flows (Dot com bubble). If a bubble happens in the future, I’d expect the market to have learned from the past and find a different justification for the next bubble.

I have no experience of options markets and so I cannot comment on them at all. I have chosen not to explore the wild and wacky mathematical landscape that is option pricing beyond the very basics of Black-Scholes and the Greeks, and I have no personal first hand experience of trading options, and so I will refrain from commenting on the option markets.

7) What Can A Retail Investor Do Knowing This?

I’ve written most of this note from my personal perspective - as a semi-skilled retail investor who has seen a glimpse of the vicissitudes of market movements and the difficulty of performing active analysis. Your conclusions might differ from mine. That said, in general I think I have painted a picture of the market being weak form and semi strong form efficient for the most part, while definitely not being strong form efficient. Violations of EMH do exist and so I cannot possibly say that the letter of EMH is true simultaneously for every market. However, I have painted a picture of violations of EMH as applying only to small amounts of stock market time and only to small fractions of the total assets under management. EMH appears to me to be mostly binding for investors outside of quantitative firms and superstar managers.

Hence I would personally believe in Fama’s suggestion that I should personally act as though the EMH were true. And being an investor who has some experience of fundamental analysis of equities, I might be willing (as Malkiel suggests) to manage around the edges of a large allocation to index funds by managing smaller positions in businesses that are within my circle of competence. My personal suggestions would be (SPY) or (VOO) for exposure to the S&P500. The benefit of this decision is that it gives me something to do to while away my free time that would otherwise be spent in utter boredom waiting for an index fund position to appreciate. Additionally, periodic rebalancing between asset classes would also have that same effect of keeping my fingers busy to avoid making stupid rash decisions if I had nothing to do. Regardless of my choice here, I would still very likely partake in equity analysis at the very minimum for the mental entertainment provided by the intellectual challenge it represents. And so I remain an avid reader of SeekingAlpha - reading the articles and the comments underneath them are a reminder of the smarts of the other market participants I would be trying to compete with.

One question remains though of tactical asset allocation. That is still an open question for me to answer. I did propose once a tactical asset allocation strategy that allocated / deallocated from the stock market and into treasury bills as a function of the equity risk premium as would be predicted by a simple regression of past CAPE ratios and their forward market returns. It seems simple in theory, however the estimate of the forward equity risk premium is fraught with a lot of noise, meaning that my formula may only be a suggestion rather than an ironclad quantitative plan. Perhaps that might be a subject of a future sketch.

data.title: Emerson Electric Set For Growth In The Second Half Of 2025
data.description:
Emerson Electric Co. (NYSE:EMR) is well-positioned for a strong upswing in the coming quarters, as management reduced tariff risk through surcharges and price increases as well as mitigation efforts across its manufacturing and supply chain footprint. With Emerson participating in large growth markets, including aerospace & defense, energy & LNG, and industrial automation & manufacturing reshoring, I believe the organization will be in a position to improve operational leverage as growth improves in the coming quarters.

As a result of the improving macroeconomic environment, I believe EMR shares should be priced at $130/share at 15.97x eFY 2026 EV/aEBITDA, providing a 16% upside potential when compared to the current price levels. I am recommending EMR shares with a Buy rating.

Emerson Electric Operations

Emerson reported a 4% increase in orders with a positive outlook across all regions during a period of flat top-line growth in Q2 2025. Despite the flat year-over-year revenue performance, Emerson realized sequential improvement across both reporting segments and all subsegments, with Intelligent Devices and Software & Control increasing by 6.5% and 5%, respectively. Management is expecting its discrete business to improve meaningfully in eH2 2025.

Management is seeing strong growth coming from significant capital investments in energy and LNG projects across EMEA, APAC, and the Americas. Global LNG capacity is expected to expand by 40% through 2030, driven by growth investments in Qatar and the US. Baker Hughes (BKR), a major player in the gas technology industry, is expecting significant growth in the coming years, with LNG FIDs projected to grow by 100MTPA from 2024-2026. Emerson has a 3-year project pipeline in energy and LNG valued at $11.4b, and was recently awarded a $375mm contract.

Emerson is also realizing strength in the aerospace & defense market with the expectations of a more favorable macroeconomic environment in eH2 2025. Carpenter Technology Corporation (CRS), a materials supplier for the aerospace & defense industry, holds similar growth expectations for the industry given the extensive backlog for aerospace and increased spending in defense. Global defense spending is expected to grow by 40% to $3.4T by 2030.

In turn, factory automation is expected to be pressured by macroeconomic uncertainty as U.S. tariff policies are adjusted through newly negotiated trade agreements. The U.S. has recently announced a new trade deal with the UK for a variety of goods in the agricultural, automotive, and steel industries. Overall, the beginning of June will be the terminus of the 90-day unilateral 10% global tariff policy following Liberation Day. Though I do not expect trade negotiations across all regions to be completed by this period, I believe the outcome will be positive for the U.S. and may provide more certainty in the market.

Accordingly, Emerson has not experienced demand declines as a result of the tariff policies in place. Management is seeing growing demand for Emerson’s Process and Hybrid businesses, while Discrete is undergoing a recovery.

As a result of the more positive outlook, management has increased their eFY 2025 guidance from 1.5-3.5% top-line growth to 4% when compared to FY 2024. Guidance for adjusted EPS was also narrowed from $5.85-6.05/share to $5.90-6.05/share. In turn, free cash flow guidance was lowered slightly to $3.1-3.2b.

In Q2 2025, Emerson completed the acquisition of AspenTech, rolling revenue and earnings across its Control Systems & Software and Test & Measurement subsegments in Software & Control. As part of the completed acquisition, management is expecting to achieve $100mm in cost synergies by 2028 by merging operating costs across the enterprise. For eFY 2025, management is expecting to achieve a $200mm run rate in cost synergies from the integration of Test & Measurement.

In Q2 2025, management made the decision to retain the Safety & Productivity business following a strategic review. For reference, Safety & Productivity includes tools for professionals, including RIGID, ProTeam, Klauke, and Greenlee.

Though the Safety & Productivity subsegment performs below the segment margin, management believes that this subsegment will be beneficial to the organization as a result of reshoring and improved domestic manufacturing. These demand trends may return the subsegment back to growth following three consecutive quarters of year-over-year declines.

Tariff Risk

Emerson, like its competitors, has certain exposures to tariffs. Accordingly, the firm has a gross exposure of $245mm in 2025, which management is expecting to be fully mitigated.

As such, most of Emerson’s risk to imposed tariffs on Chinese imports was mitigated in 2018 following the initial trade war between the U.S. and China.

In 2024, 19% of Emerson’s cost of goods sold were imported as raw materials or semi-finished products. 80% of Emerson’s imports from Mexico qualify for USMCA exemptions.

As a result of the cost impacts from tariffs, Emerson is actively mitigating risks through surcharges and price increases, as well as reconfiguring its global manufacturing footprint while regionalizing its supply chain.

Emerson Electric Financial Position

For Q3 2025, I’m forecasting Emerson to generate $4.4b in net sales, with an adjusted EPS of $1.48/share. This would place eFY 2025 revenue at $18b and adjusted EPS at $6.00/share. This forecast assumes an improved business environment going into eH2 2025, with growth primarily occurring in Q4 2025.

Looking ahead, management will be allocating a certain proportion of free cash flow to paying down its debt position. Emerson exited Q2 2025 with $1.9b in cash & equivalent and $14b in debt on the balance sheet for a leverage ratio of 2.75x net debt/EBITDA. Management is targeting reducing its debt position to 2x in the coming years.

Risks Related to Emerson Electric

Bull Case

Emerson is participating in multiple megatrends that may drive growth in the coming years, including increased spending across aerospace & defense, power & LNG capacity, and manufacturing reshoring and industrial automation.

In addition to this, Emerson has positioned its operations to mitigate most of the impact from the current tariff environment through price increases as well as mitigation efforts across its manufacturing & supply chain footprints.

Though shareholder returns are relatively capped for the duration of the year as management focuses on allocating free cash flow to paying down debt, Emerson may turn out to be in a stronger position as it exits eFY 2025 with a stronger balance sheet and more capacity for growth, providing more flexibility for shareholder returns.

Bear Case

Trade negotiations are an ongoing concern, with early June being the terminus for the 90-day tariff pause following Liberation Day. Though I suspect international trade will benefit from trade negotiations, there may be regions that don’t meet a positive trade balance with the U.S., potentially leading to a return of heightened tariffs. This could potentially add certain risks to Emerson’s growth path in the near-term, resulting in slower growth in the coming quarters.

Valuation & Shareholder Value

In Q2 2025, management lowered their guidance for shareholder returns from $3.2b as guided in Q1 2025 to $2.3b. This translates to $1.1b in share repurchases, which were completed in H1 2025, and $1.2b in dividends. This was likely the result of the decision to retain the Safety & Productivity subsegment, the completion of the acquisition of AspenTech, as well as paying down its debt position. Looking ahead, management is expecting $2.5b of available free cash flow over the next two years that can be allocated to share repurchases and additional M&A activity.

EMR shares are currently trading at 16.82x EV/aEBITDA, a relative discount to its industrial automation peer average of 19.07x. On a TTM basis, a 19.07x multiple would price EMR shares at $130/share.

I believe EMR shares should be priced at $130/share at 15.97x eFY 2026 EV/aEBITDA, providing a 16% upside potential when compared to the current price levels. I am recommending EMR shares with a BUY rating.

Using the model: the valuation table above references my financial forecast in the firm’s “financial position” section and ties it to the stock’s historical trading premiums. The trading premium array is derived through the normal operating cycle, with the blue-sky scenario being the stock’s peak multiple and the gray-sky scenario being the lowest point. The target multiple aims for the midpoint, or the most likely trading range for the company’s stock. The trading multiples from there are set to a probability factor based on the likelihood of the stock trading at that premium based on its historical presence. From there, the trading multiple is tied to the probability factor to derive its relative market cap and relative multiple.

data.title: MCI: Premium Valuation Amidst Uncertain Outlook
data.description:
Overview

As we remain in a higher interest rate environment, I am constantly on the lookout for attractive opportunities to capitalize. Barings Corporate Investors (NYSE:MCI) operates as a closed end fund that aims to generate attractive earnings from its portfolio of diverse debt investments. The main benefit of a fund like this is its ability to harness the higher interest rate environment and produce high income for shareholders. I previously covered MCI and issued a hold rating due to the outlook on interest rate cuts at the time. Since the time of my last coverage, the economic outlook has shifted and MCI has also posted an updated report for 2024, so I wanted to revisit this fund.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that the price has increased by about 6.4%. When including all distributions paid out to shareholders over the same time frame, the total return jumps up to 17%. MCI has a generous starting dividend yield of approximately 7.8% when based off of the latest declared quarterly distribution. This higher yield makes it an attractive choice for investors that are looking for a source of supplemental income. The income levels remain elevated since interest rates are still high and depending on the remainder of 2025, investors may be able to continue collecting higher income.

MCI has an inception dating back to 1971, making it one of the longest operating closed end funds. The fund is managed by Barings and has an expense ratio of 1.65%. The fund's historical performance proves that the underlying strategy is efficient at generating positive total returns. However, this has caused MCI to now trade in the premium to NAV territory and this makes it difficult to get an attractive entry point. Additionally, the fund can be vulnerable to lower earnings if interest rates are cut throughout the year. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying strategy and investment methodology that MCI implements to generate its earnings.

Fund Strategy

The fund generates its earnings through various different income-focused debt vehicles. According to the latest reporting, 72.4% of the portfolio is concentrated to private bank loans. This is followed by exposure to private/restricted equity and private debt investments, accounting for 13.2% and 5.7% of assets respectively. In order to get better clarity of the specific sector breakdown of the fund, I had to dig into the details of the annual report. The breakdown by the top sectors is as follows:

  • Technology: 23.42% of the portfolio.
  • Industrial: 16.85% of the portfolio.
  • Healthcare: 9.39% of the portfolio.

MCI does have varying exposure to other sectors like Media, Aerospace, and Consumer Cyclical just to name a few. However, the top three sectors make up nearly 50% of the fund's total assets, so we can assume that these will have the greatest impact on the daily price movement and overall total return potential.

Additionally, 70.8% of the fund's debt investments are structured on a floating rate basis. This is the magic behind MCI's ability to generate higher income because a higher interest rate environment directly translates to the fund's ability to collect higher interest income from its underlying borrowers. However, the inclusion of a floating rate structure can come with a bit of a caveat. A higher cost of debt can negatively impact the operating margins for borrowers and lead to a higher amount of defaults.

Risk Profile

As part of the fund's strategy, it utilizes a conservative amount of leverage in an effort to amplify returns when an opportunity presents itself. According to the annual report, leverage is usually equivalent to 11% of assets, which I consider to be fairly low risk. In addition, the fund typically maintains liquidity in the form of cash that sits around 4.2% of total assets. However, the inclusion of leverage automatically increases the risk profile because there's a chance that leverage can amplify losses if allocated towards a poor investment. The use of leverage also means that the fund has to account for interest payments in its operating expenses.

The annual report doesn't indicate the specific credit rating breakdown for each holding. Unfortunately, sources like CEF Connect also seem to have an incomplete breakdown as it states that all of MCI's debt investments are not rated. However, we can assume that a portion of the investments within MCI have credit ratings that are below investment grade, since the fund offers a superior yield. The general rule of thumb with debt investments is the higher the yield, the higher the risk of the investment.

If MCI has a significant exposure to investments with poor credit ratings, the fund may experience a higher rate of defaults. S&P Global estimates that the rate of leveraged loan defaults can see an uptick to 1.6% by December of 2025 in its base case scenario. However, the pessimistic scenario sees defaults growing to 3% by December of 2025. I believe that we are currently somewhere in the middle of the two scenarios since interest rates remain high.

The Fed previously stated there could still be two interest rate cuts throughout 2025 but they would be two small 25-basis-point reductions. However, the Fed has most recently left rates unchanged because of uncertainty in the markets. Investors are still trying to determine the ripple effects of President Trump's tariff proposals, inflationary data still remains higher than anticipated, and the unemployment rate continues to trend upward over the last twelve months. Therefore, the Fed remains observant before making any decisions. Depending on which direction inflation goes, I believe there may even be a chance that there are no cuts or increases over the course of 2025, which could lead to higher defaults.

Updated Valuation

Since MCI operates as a closed end fund, the price of the fund can trade at a difference from the actual value of the underlying assets. As a result, MCI currently trades at a premium to NAV of about 15.8%. Looking back over the last ten years, the current premium remains on the higher end of its historical range, indicating that now is not the best time for accumulation of shares. The premium is likely driven up by the fact that interest rates are higher and MCI has been able to generate elevated levels of net investment income.

In order to get better clarity, I reviewed the annual report for 2024. Net investment income amounted to $1.71 per share while net realized gains landed at $0.04 per share. Looking back on prior years, we can see how net investment income levels have reached new heights. However, net realized gains were barely positive, which reinforces the idea that a higher rate environment can make it more difficult for underlying investments to grow in valuations. Something that stands out to me is MCI's consistent NAV growth over the years. The fund has been able to successfully out earn distributions and regain its positive NAV momentum from its pandemic lows. For reference, here are the year-end NAV amounts:

  • 2020: $15.04 per share
  • 2021: $16.68 per share
  • 2022: $16.37 per share
  • 2023: $16.77 per share
  • 2024: $16.84 per share

The fund's success in growing its NAV is relevant when measuring the performance over a long period of time. For instance, MCI's price has increased by more than 25% over the last decade, while the inclusion of distributions boosts the total return above 167%.

Even when looking at the price movement alone, MCI's share price has increased by 753% since inception. MCI is one of those needle in a haystack type of closed end funds that has been able to deliver capital appreciation and consistent distributions. There are a lot of debt investment focused closed end funds that see their NAV deteriorate over time due to overly generous distributions policies and poor strategies. So MCI's long term track record proves that the strategy is efficient through different market cycles.

Distribution

The latest declared quarterly distribution sits at $0.39 per share, bringing the current dividend yield around 7.8%. Assuming a similar payout over the next few quarters, we can multiply this by four and get an estimated annual payout of $1.56 per share. Referring back to the same annual report, MCI hasn't had trouble out earning this amount when market conditions are positive. For instance, here are the annual earnings amounts:

  • 2020: $0.76 per share
  • 2021: $2.60 per share
  • 2022: $0.71 per share
  • 2023: $1.82 per share
  • 2024: $1.75 per share

Looking back over the last five years, MCI has fully supported the distribution with earnings more often than not. The fund didn't generate enough earnings in 2020 due to the initial market slide caused by covid.
